claim_chain: Substantial differences exist among countries in the proportion of people who reported poor or fair mental health
Comorbidity rates are overall high, suggesting an essential role for primary care
Major differences in use of mental healthcare are strongly associated with national income
People with poor mental health almost universally report worse quality and user experience in accessing general health services
These findings should prompt health systems to re-assess their services to better serve this growing patient group

typical_pattern: State key finding with quantitative evidence
Compare to existing literature or other countries
Discuss implications for health systems or policy

likely_priorities: Validity of self-reported mental health as proxy for clinical diagnosis
Comparability across countries with different cultural norms around mental health reporting
Temporal relationship and causality between mental health and chronic conditions
Generalizability given survey methodology differences across countries
likely_attacks: Results based on self-reported mental health rather than clinical diagnoses
Question wording and time horizon differ across studies making direct comparison difficult
Cultural norms shape how individuals perceive and articulate mental health issues
Depressive symptoms may wax and wane and can resolve without clinical treatment
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly states 'A study limitation is that results are based on self-reported mental health rather than clinical diagnoses' in the abstract. The discussion acknowledges that 'report of mental health conditions is shaped by cultural norms and societal context' and that 'question wording and the time horizon for measuring prevalence of mental health conditions differ across studies'. The authors also note that 'Depressive symptoms may wax and wane in the general population and can resolve without clinical treatment; thus a portion of our respondents may not require mental healthcare', suggesting awareness that their outcome measure may not directly correspond to treatment need.

our estimates broadly comport with the few available measures in the same time frame
These findings should prompt health systems to re-assess their services

phrase_evidence.phrase: This study of 18 low-, middle-, and high-income countries yielded several key findings
substantial differences among countries in the proportion of people who reported poor or fair mental health
comorbidity rates are overall high, suggesting an essential role for primary care
major differences in use of mental healthcare; these are strongly associated with national income
people with poor mental health almost universally report worse quality and user experience
prevalence was unrelated to national income, suggesting that factors other than economic resources or living standards are important drivers
report of mental health conditions is shaped by cultural norms and societal context
our estimates broadly comport with the few available measures in the same time frame
which may account for their lower prevalence
This points to the need for health systems to pursue a range of strategies
This relationship between depression and chronic conditions is likely bidirectional
underlines the need for health systems to screen people
This has important implications for treatment success
This is not a direct measure of treatment access
Depressive symptoms may wax and wane in the general population and can resolve without clinical treatment
Our country-level findings on the use of mental healthcare are consistent with other research
One likely major factor is the shortage of mental health professionals
This suggests that repeated contact with health systems can promote uptake of needed mental healthcare
This may be a combination of greater need for all medical services
These findings should prompt health systems to re-assess their services to better serve this growing patient group
A study limitation is that results are based on self-reported mental health rather than clinical diagnoses
Weighted percent of respondents who reported poor or fair on a five-point scale
Error bars represent 95% CI
Distribution of respondents reporting poor or fair mental health by age group
Each figure icon represents 2% of the population

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers were included. The profile is based entirely on the provided discussion, abstract, and figure legends.
